text: The surgeon places two 10 mm ports in the posterior axillary line at the fifth and ninth intercostal spaces, and two 5 mm ports in the mid axillary line at the third and seventh intercostal spaces, marking the standard port placement for the procedure.
The surgical setup includes a camera port labeled 'C', two working ports labeled 'W1' and 'W2', and an assistant port labeled 'A', all positioned for the esophagectomy procedure.
The surgeon begins dissection at the inferior pulmonary ligament, retracting the lung anteriorly to expose the area and cutting through the ligament.

The surgeon aims to remove all fibro-fatty tissue along with the lymph nodes, while defining the inferior pulmonary vein, which is the first structure to be identified in this esophagectomy procedure.
The surgeon uses traction and counter traction to manipulate the specimen, which includes several small lymph nodes visible within the surgical field.
The assistant provides traction by pulling the lung anteriorly, while the surgeon applies counter traction by retracting the specimen posteriorly, facilitating the dissection process.
The surgeon identifies and dissects along the avascular plane between the inferior pulmonary vein and the specimen, ensuring that all fibrofatty tissue is included as part of the specimen.
The surgeon carefully dissects tissue away from the inferior pulmonary vein and pericardium, utilizing an avascular plane that extends from the hiatus to the subcarinal lymph node basin.
The surgeon uses sharp dissection to guide the specimen towards the opposite pleura, carefully navigating the avascular plane between the inferior pulmonary vein and the specimen.
The surgeon uses a monopolar hook to perform precise dissection, maintaining a dry surgical field while separating tissue layers.

The surgeon demonstrates the concept of mesoesophageal accession by dissecting anteriorly, taking all tissue with the specimen while preserving important structures as landmarks. Posteriorly, fibrophytic tissue is also taken with the specimen, maintaining a clear avascular plane.

The surgeon continues dissection along the pericardium up to the hiatus, incorporating all tissue with the specimen. The nodal basin above the hiatus is addressed, ensuring no lymph nodes are left behind despite the presence of the inferior vena cava. The dissection progresses towards the contralateral pleura, which becomes visible.
The contralateral pleura becomes visible, marking the limit of the contralateral dissection during the esophagectomy procedure.
The surgeon carefully dissects around the inferior pulmonary vein on the left side, safeguarding this structure while addressing two important nodal basins situated above and below it.

The surgeon cuts the pleura over the right bronchus, exposing the area for further dissection. The surgical field is kept dry to enhance visualization of small and important structures in this region.

The surgeon removes lymph nodes from station 108 adjacent to the right bronchus, incorporating them with the specimen. The assistant retracts the right bronchus downward and anteriorly, while the surgeon provides counter traction by lifting the tissue posteriorly.
The surgeon applies counter-traction to reveal a clear plane of dissection, facilitating the separation of tissue layers.
The surgeon identifies two important structures over the right bronchus: the vagus nerve, which is a proprioceptive nerve that must be preserved for optimal post-operative recovery.

The surgeon selectively cuts the vagus nerve branches entering the esophagus while preserving its bronchial branches. The right bronchial artery is clearly visualized, and its branches leading to the esophagus are carefully dissected.

The surgeon separates the esophagus from the trachea, noting the absence of fat between these structures. Dissection is performed flush to the esophagus, parallel to the trachea, with the left border of the trachea serving as a visual landmark for guidance.
The surgeon defines and delineates the left side of the bronchus, which is clearly visible. After completing the dissection along the tracheobronchial tree, the focus shifts to posterior dissection.
During the posterior dissection, the surgeon carefully stays flush to the azygos vein while making a posterior incision on the mediastinal pleura, aiming to remove all fibrophytic tissue.

The surgeon moves away from the ozygous vein to define the prevascular fascia, which covers the descending aorta. The fat outside the prevascular fascia contains the thoracic duct, serving as a useful anatomical landmark for thoracic duct clipping.

The surgeon confidently clips the thoracic duct and its subsidiary branches while dissecting the prevascular fascia. The descending aorta becomes visible as it emerges from the prevascular fascia, and the fat containing the thoracic duct is lifted away from the descending aorta and azygous vein.
The surgeon clips and cuts the thoracic duct, then continues with the posterior dissection, carefully retracting all tissues away from the descending aorta.

The surgeon approaches the contralateral pleura, taking the station 1 and 2 lymph nodes with the specimen. A clear plane of separation is visible between the mesoesophagus and the left pleura. The pleura remains closed at this phase, but if accidentally opened, the window is enlarged at the end of the thoracoscopic phase to avoid placing a contralateral intercostal drainage tube.
After completing the infra-axiacus dissection, the surgeon transitions to the supra-axiacus dissection, focusing on the right side of the surgical field.

The surgeon begins the recurrent laryngeal lymph node dissection by identifying the superior vena cava and making a pleural incision along it. The dissection continues towards the apex of the chest to expose and define the right subclavian artery, around which the right recurrent laryngeal lymph node is located.

The surgeon identifies the vagus nerve, subclavian artery, and the right recurrent laryngeal nerve, which arises from the vagus nerve and encircles the subclavian artery. The specimen is then separated from the trachea as the dissection progresses towards the neck.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to carefully dissect along the right side of the vagus nerve, ensuring the active blade is tilted away from the membranous portion of the trachea to prevent accidental injury.

The right vagus nerve is visible crossing the trachea and extending towards the right subclavian artery. The station 106 tracheobronchial lymph node is located between the fork formed by the trachea and the vagus nerve.

The surgeon performs sharp dissection to remove fibro-fatty tissue along with the right recurrent laryngeal lymph node group, ensuring the area remains dry for clear visibility. The right recurrent laryngeal nerve is visible, and pauses are taken to allow the active blade to cool down, preventing thermal injury.
The surgeon uses scissors to carefully define and delineate the fibers of the nerve, ensuring precise separation and identification of the nerve structures.

The surgeon identifies and cuts nerve fibers entering the specimen to define the main trunk of the right recurrent laryngeal nerve. The right recurrent laryngeal nerve is clearly visible, with posteriorly situated lymph nodes included in the right lymph node packet.

The surgeon uses a combination of an energy source and scissors to carefully dissect around the recurrent laryngeal nerve, remaining vigilant for any anomalous branches that may arise one or two centimeters above the expected location.

The surgeon carefully encircles the subclavian artery while defining the upper limit of the right recurrent laryngeal lymph node dissection, marked by the inferior thyroid artery. The esophageal branches of the inferior thyroid artery are visible, indicating the dissection limit. The entire lymph node packet is removed as a single specimen for oncological safety.

The surgeon completes the en masse removal of the specimen, including the station 106 recurrent lymph node, concluding the right recurrent dissection. The procedure then transitions to the left side recurrent dissection.

The surgeon separates the specimen from the left side of the trachea using a tracheal dip maneuver, removing fibro-fatty tissue along the left tracheal side. The visceral fascia covering the left recurrent laryngeal lymph node is noted. The left recurrent laryngeal nerve is visible, and the AP window lymph node is gradually separated.

The surgeon carefully dissects lymph nodes from the trachea and the main pulmonary artery (MPA), noting the single file arrangement of the AP window lymph nodes. As one lymph node is removed, a chain of additional lymph nodes becomes visible, requiring diligent removal.
The surgeon performs suprazygous lymph node dissection, with the descending aorta visible posteriorly and the trachea anteriorly. The AP window is cleared of lymph nodes.
The final view shows the specimen with attached lymph nodes, including the left and right recurrent laryngeal nerves, at the end of the esophagectomy procedure.

The surgeon completes the esophagus transaction, preparing it for anastomosis by separating the lymph nodes from the esophagus. The specimen is pulled from the abdomen, marking the transition from the abdominal phase to the thoracic phase of the Ivor Lewis esophagectomy.

The surgeon converts the seventh intercostal space port into a utility incision to extract the esophageal specimen. The gastric conduit is prepared by applying multiple staplers, ensuring the entire fundus is included for a good gastric margin. The upper end of the esophagus is prepared for anastomosis, and the gastric conduit is thoroughly washed internally. A side-to-side stapled anastomosis is performed using an endoGI stapler, visible in the Iverlewis esophagectomy procedure.

The surgeon ensures at least six to seven centimeters of mobile esophagus for a tension-free anastomosis. After applying the initial stapler, additional staplers are used to complete the entrotomy during the Ivor Lewis esophagectomy.
The final image of the surgical field shows a negative suction drain placed to drain the gastric conduit, marking the completion of the esophagectomy procedure.
